books.title: I Hate the Ivy League: Riffs and Rants on Elite Education
books.author: Malcolm Gladwell
books.description: Malcolm Gladwell turns his signature contrarian lens on elite American higher education — questioning whether Ivy League schools actually produce better outcomes than their reputations suggest, or whether prestige has become a self-perpetuating myth that serves the powerful more than the talented.
discussionPrompts: Did Gladwell's argument change how you think about elite university prestige? Were you convinced?
Is the Ivy League admissions process a meritocracy, or something else entirely?
Gladwell argues that many high-achieving students would be better off at non-elite schools. Do you agree?
What role does social capital — who you know — play in the value of an elite degree?
How does this book apply to the education systems outside the US, including in our own backgrounds?
Is Gladwell being provocative for the sake of it, or does the data genuinely support his claims?
spoilerPrompts: Which specific argument or data point in the book surprised you most?
Did the book change any decisions you'd make for your own children or family regarding education?

books.title: The Correspondent
books.author: Virginia Evans
books.description: A novel selected by Dibjot Singh for the February 2026 session.
discussionPrompts: What drew you into the story in the opening chapters?
How does the author develop the central character's voice and perspective?
What themes did you notice running through the novel?
Which relationship in the book felt most authentic or complicated?
How did the ending land for you — did it feel earned?
Would you recommend this to someone outside the club? Why or why not?
spoilerPrompts: What was the moment that changed everything for you as a reader?
Looking back, what did the title mean to you after finishing?

books.title: 21 Lessons for the 21st Century
books.author: Yuval Noah Harari
books.description: Yuval Noah Harari, author of Sapiens and Homo Deus, focuses on the present — addressing the most urgent questions of our time: how do we navigate a world reshaped by technology, nationalism, religion, and misinformation? A sweeping, thought-provoking guide to the forces defining the 21st century.
discussionPrompts: Which of Harari's 21 lessons hit closest to home for you personally?
Do you think AI and automation will ultimately improve human lives, or will the disruption outweigh the benefit?
Harari is quite critical of nationalism and religion as coping frameworks. Did you find that fair?
How do you personally deal with information overload and media manipulation in your daily life?
The book argues that the most important skill for the future is the ability to reinvent yourself. Do you agree?
Is Harari too pessimistic, appropriately alarmed, or not alarmed enough?
spoilerPrompts: The final lesson is about meditation and self-knowledge. Did that ending surprise you given the book's political sweep?
Which prediction in the book do you think is most likely to age badly — and which most likely to prove true?

books.title: Murder at the Patel Motel
books.author: Maulik Pancholy
books.description: The story is a comic murder mystery centered on Milan Patel, a gay New York City event planner who returns to his family’s run-down motel in small-town Montana and gets pulled into a murder investigation after his father is found dead in the pool. It has been described as Agatha Christie–style, with humor, family secrets, and emotional stakes mixed into the mystery. It was created/co-written by Maulik Pancholy and released by Audible in June 2025 as a full-cast audio original.
discussionPrompts: How does Pancholy use the motel setting to explore themes of belonging and the immigrant experience?
Ansh is caught between two worlds — how does that tension play out through the story?
What did the book say about small-town America and how outsiders are perceived?
How does the mystery plot serve (or distract from) the deeper emotional story?
Were there moments of humour that also carried a sharper point?
What does 'home' mean to Ansh by the end — and did that resonate with you?
spoilerPrompts: Who did you suspect, and when did you figure out the real answer?
Did the resolution of the mystery feel satisfying alongside the emotional arc?
